notes.note: The college of augurs, the priesthood of state diviners; a vacancy in 50 BC set off the contest in which Caelius backed Antony, earning Appius's and L. Domitius's enmity.
Appius's fellow censor of 50 BC, L. Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus (Caesar's father-in-law); Caelius spitefully sought a favour from him rather than from Appius.

notes.anchor: the Lex Scantinia (a statute against unnatural offences)
notes.note: An old Roman law penalizing homosexual offences against freeborn youths; Appius's faction indicted Caelius under it, whereupon Caelius gleefully counter-charged Appius the censor under the same law.
As curule aedile in 50 BC Caelius was responsible for staging public games (the ludi circenses); his opponents timed the summons to humiliate him at the height of his own festival.

notes.anchor: the chapel that stands in his house
notes.note: A sacellum (private shrine) in Appius's house; Caelius, exploiting the censor's own jurisdiction over religious propriety, formally demanded that Appius account for it.
